questions.q: VFR cruising altitude for magnetic course 090 degrees at 7500 ft MSL (more than 3000 AGL)?
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.159: MC 0 to 179 degrees (eastbound) = odd thousands plus 500. MC 090 is eastbound so valid altitudes are 3500, 5500, 7500, 9500 ft. 7500 ft is correct.

questions.q: VFR cruising altitude rule for westbound flight at MC 270 degrees?
choices: Odd thousands plus 500 ft
Even thousands plus 500 ft
Any altitude above 3000 AGL
Even thousands only
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.159: MC 180 to 359 degrees (westbound) = even thousands plus 500 ft. MC 270 is westbound so use 4500, 6500, 8500 ft etc.

questions.q: TVMDC formula — what does each letter represent?
choices: Temperature Visibility Moisture Drag Ceiling
True course plus or minus Variation equals Magnetic plus or minus Deviation equals Compass course
Turbulence Velocity Moisture Dew Cloud
Time Voltage Magnetic deviation CAS
questions.explanation: TVMDC: True course +/- Variation = Magnetic course +/- Deviation = Compass course. East is subtract, West is add. Memory: True Virgins Make Dull Company.

questions.q: VFR day fuel reserve requirement?
choices: 20 minutes at cruise
30 minutes at cruise
45 minutes at cruise
1 hour at cruise
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.151(a)(1): Must have enough fuel to fly to the first intended landing plus at least 30 minutes at normal cruising speed during daylight.

questions.q: VFR night fuel reserve requirement?
choices: 20 minutes
30 minutes
45 minutes
1 hour
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.151(a)(2): Night VFR requires fuel to reach the first intended landing plus at least 45 minutes at normal cruising speed.

questions.q: Solid magenta circle around an airport on a sectional chart indicates?
choices: Class D airspace
Class C airspace
Class E airspace at the surface
Class B inner ring
questions.explanation: A solid magenta circle indicates Class C airspace. Class D is a segmented (dashed) blue circle. Class E at the surface is a dashed magenta circle.

questions.q: Sectional aeronautical chart scale?
questions.explanation: Sectional charts are published at 1:500000 scale, approximately 1 inch equals 6.86 nm. VFR Terminal Area Charts (TAC) for Class B areas are 1:250000.

questions.q: TAS 120 kts with a direct 20-kt headwind — groundspeed?
choices: 140 kts
120 kts
100 kts
110 kts
questions.explanation: Groundspeed = TAS minus headwind component for a direct headwind. 120 minus 20 equals 100 kts.

questions.q: Maximum Elevation Figure (MEF) on a sectional chart represents?
choices: The highest terrain elevation in the quadrangle
The highest obstacle plus 100 ft vertical clearance rounded up to the next 100 ft
The minimum safe VFR cruising altitude
The radar coverage floor altitude
questions.explanation: The MEF includes the highest obstacle in a lat/lon quadrangle plus 100 ft of vertical clearance, rounded up to the next 100 ft increment.

questions.q: Sectional aeronautical charts are revised how often?
choices: Every 3 months
Every 6 months
Every 12 months
Every 56 days like instrument charts
questions.explanation: VFR Sectional charts are revised every 6 months. World Aeronautical Charts (WAC) are revised annually. TACs are also revised every 6 months.

questions.q: VOR navigation frequency range?
choices: 329 to 335 MHz
108.0 to 111.95 MHz only
questions.explanation: VOR navigation frequencies are 108.0 to 117.95 MHz. The range 108.0 to 111.95 MHz is shared with ILS localizers (odd tenths). Comm frequencies are 118.0 to 136.975 MHz.

questions.q: True course 045 degrees, magnetic variation 10 degrees East — magnetic course?
choices: 055 degrees
045 degrees
035 degrees
050 degrees
questions.explanation: TVMDC: TC +/- Variation = MC. East variation means subtract. MC = 045 minus 10 = 035 degrees.

questions.q: What does RAIM stand for and why is it important?
choices: Radar Altitude Indication Monitor — used for altitude alerts
Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring — checks GPS signal integrity; required for IFR non-WAAS approaches
Radio Altitude Instrument Module — backup gyro system
Real-time Airspace Information Module — for airspace alerts
questions.explanation: RAIM uses redundant GPS satellites to monitor signal integrity autonomously. For IFR GPS approaches without WAAS, a RAIM check must confirm adequate signal integrity before departure.

questions.q: Is a VFR flight plan legally required?
choices: Yes for all cross-country flights
Yes for any flight over 50 nm
No — strongly recommended for search and rescue but not legally required by the FARs
Yes in Class E airspace
questions.explanation: VFR flight plans are NOT required by the FARs. However, they are strongly recommended as a search-and-rescue tool. File with FSS and close on arrival.

questions.q: You cross a checkpoint 3 minutes late at 120 kts groundspeed. How far behind are you?
choices: 3 nm
5 nm
6 nm
2 nm
questions.explanation: At 120 kts groundspeed the aircraft covers 2 nm per minute. Three minutes late = 2 times 3 = 6 nm behind the planned position.

questions.q: What is dead reckoning navigation?
choices: Navigation using only radio navigation aids
Calculating current position from a known starting point using heading, airspeed or groundspeed, time, and wind — without external position fixes
Navigation by GPS only
Navigation using only visual ground references
questions.explanation: Dead reckoning calculates current position from a known starting point by advancing that position using estimated groundspeed, heading corrected for wind, and elapsed time.

questions.q: What is magnetic variation and how does easterly variation affect conversion from true to magnetic course?
choices: Magnetic variation is compass deviation — no effect on heading
Magnetic variation is the angle between true north and magnetic north. East variation: subtract from true course to get magnetic course.
East variation means add to get magnetic course
Variation only applies east of the prime meridian
questions.explanation: Magnetic variation is the angular difference between true north and magnetic north shown by isogonic lines on charts. East variation: subtract (TC minus variation = MC). West variation: add.

questions.q: ATIS — what information does it provide and where is the frequency found?
choices: Air Traffic Information System — on sectional chart legend
Automated Terminal Information Service — provides current weather, active runways, and NOTAMs; frequency on sectional chart or Chart Supplement
Air Traffic ILS System — for instrument approaches only
Airport Traffic Identification System — for towered airports only
questions.explanation: ATIS provides continuous recorded information about weather, runways, NOTAMs, and other terminal information. Frequencies are found on sectional charts near the airport symbol and in the Chart Supplement.

questions.q: True altitude vs pressure altitude — what is the difference?
choices: They are the same thing
True altitude is actual height above MSL; pressure altitude is indicated altitude when altimeter is set to 29.92 in Hg
Pressure altitude is corrected for temperature; true altitude is shown on the altimeter
True altitude is AGL; pressure altitude is MSL
questions.explanation: True altitude is actual height above mean sea level. Pressure altitude is the altitude indicated when the altimeter is set to 29.92 in Hg. Density altitude = pressure altitude corrected for temperature.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the wind correction angle (WCA)?
choices: To correct for magnetic variation
To adjust the aircraft heading into the wind so the actual track over the ground matches the desired course despite crosswind
To correct for runway slope during landing
To adjust airspeed for wind during cruise
questions.explanation: The wind correction angle adjusts the heading to compensate for crosswind drift, so the actual ground track matches the desired course.

questions.q: What is a VOR radial and how is it defined?
choices: A radial is a signal transmitted TO the VOR from an aircraft
A radial is a magnetic bearing FROM the VOR station — the 090 radial is the line extending east from the VOR. Aircraft position is described by which radial they are ON, not flying.
A radial is a true bearing from the VOR
A radial is a GPS direct-to course
questions.explanation: VOR radials are magnetic bearings extending outward FROM the VOR station. The 090 radial extends east (090 degrees magnetic) from the station. If you are on the 090 radial, you are east of the VOR. Radials are always FROM the station regardless of which direction you are flying.

questions.q: What is a VOR station service volume and why does it matter for flight planning?
choices: The service volume is unlimited for all VOR stations

VOR stations have defined service volumes (T=Terminal, L=Low Altitude, H=High Altitude) within which reliable signal reception is guaranteed — planning should use VORs within their service volume for the altitude being flown
VOR service volume only applies to IFR flight
All VOR stations have the same service volume
questions.explanation: VOR stations are classified by their Standard Service Volume (SSV): T (Terminal) 25 nm up to 12,000 ft AGL; L (Low altitude) 40 nm up to 18,000 ft; H (High altitude) 130 nm above 14,500 ft. Using a VOR outside its service volume may result in unreliable signal reception.

questions.q: What is deviation and how does it differ from variation?
choices: Deviation and variation are the same — both refer to magnetic declination

Variation is the difference between true north and magnetic north (isogonic lines on charts). Deviation is the error in a specific compass caused by local magnetic influences in the aircraft — shown on the compass deviation card.
Deviation is the difference between true north and geographic north
Variation is the compass error; deviation is the chart correction
questions.explanation: Magnetic variation is the angular difference between true north and magnetic north — a fixed geographic value shown by isogonic lines on charts. Deviation is the error in a specific aircraft's magnetic compass caused by magnetic fields from aircraft systems, engines, and structure — unique to each aircraft and shown on the compass deviation card.

questions.q: When would you use Vx immediately after liftoff during a short field takeoff?
choices: Never — always use Vy after takeoff
When obstacles are present on the departure end — Vx provides the greatest altitude gain over horizontal distance, clearing obstacles
When the runway is soft or wet
When departing from a high-elevation airport
questions.explanation: Vx is used when obstacles must be cleared on the departure end of the runway. It provides the maximum altitude gained per unit of horizontal distance traveled, trading slower climb rate for steeper climb angle. Transition to Vy once the obstacle is cleared.

questions.q: What is the VFR cruising altitude rule and at what point does it begin to apply?
choices: Applies at all altitudes
Applies only above 18,000 ft MSL
Applies when more than 3,000 ft above the surface (AGL) — not MSL. Below 3,000 AGL there is no cruising altitude requirement.
Applies above 1,000 ft MSL
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.159: The VFR cruising altitude rule applies when flying at more than 3,000 feet ABOVE THE SURFACE (AGL), not above MSL. In a mountainous area where the terrain is at 8,000 ft MSL, the rule begins at 11,000 ft MSL (8,000 + 3,000).

questions.q: You are flying a magnetic course of 355 degrees at 8,500 ft MSL, more than 3,000 ft AGL. Is this a legal VFR cruising altitude?
choices: Yes — any altitude above 3,000 AGL is fine

No — magnetic course 355 degrees is northbound (0-179 degrees range) so odd thousands plus 500 should be used. 8,500 is even thousands plus 500 and would be used for westbound (180-359 degrees). Correct altitude would be 7,500 or 9,500 ft.
Yes — 8,500 is acceptable for any heading
No — you should be at 9,000 ft
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.159: MC 355 degrees is northbound (0-179 degrees range), which requires ODD thousands plus 500 ft: 3,500; 5,500; 7,500; 9,500 etc. 8,500 ft is the even + 500 altitude used for westbound/southbound (180-359 degree range). This is a common exam trap.

questions.q: What is pilotage and how is it used in VFR navigation?
choices: Pilotage is automated navigation using GPS
Pilotage is navigation by visual reference to landmarks — the pilot identifies ground features and compares them to the aeronautical chart to confirm position and navigate along a route
Pilotage is navigation using only radio aids
Pilotage is the process of taxiing at an unfamiliar airport
questions.explanation: Pilotage is visual navigation by reference to landmarks. The pilot identifies cities, rivers, highways, railroads, lakes, towers, and other features visible from the air and matches them to the aeronautical chart to confirm position and navigate. Often combined with dead reckoning for cross-country flights.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a flight log or navigation log on a cross-country flight?
choices: It is only required for IFR flights

A navigation log documents the planned route, checkpoints, headings, distances, estimated groundspeeds, fuel burns, and estimated times enroute — used to track progress and identify when off course during VFR XC flights
Navigation logs are obsolete now that GPS is available
A navigation log is required to be filed with the FAA
questions.explanation: A navigation log (nav log or flight log) is a planning document that organizes all cross-country flight data: checkpoints, true/magnetic course, wind correction angle, true/magnetic heading, compass heading, distance, groundspeed, estimated time enroute, and fuel burn. It is essential for situational awareness during the flight.

questions.q: An altimeter set to 29.42 in Hg when the current altimeter setting is 29.92 in Hg. How does this affect the indicated altitude compared to true altitude?
choices: No effect — altimeters are self-correcting
The altimeter will read approximately 500 feet LOWER than actual altitude — the pilot is higher than the altimeter indicates. Remember: high to low, look out below.
The altimeter will read 500 feet higher than actual altitude
The altimeter will be exactly correct but the VSI will be affected
questions.explanation: Each 0.10 in Hg change in altimeter setting corresponds to approximately 100 ft of altitude error. Setting the altimeter too low (29.42 vs. 29.92 = 0.50 in Hg too low) causes the altimeter to read approximately 500 ft LOW — the aircraft is 500 ft HIGHER than indicated. High to low, look out below.

questions.q: What is the difference between indicated airspeed (IAS), calibrated airspeed (CAS), and true airspeed (TAS)?
choices: They are all the same — different names for the same measurement

IAS is what the airspeed indicator shows. CAS is IAS corrected for instrument and position errors (published in POH). TAS is CAS corrected for altitude and temperature — the actual speed through the air mass. TAS increases with altitude at a constant IAS.
IAS is only used on the ground; TAS is used in the air
CAS is used only for IFR operations
questions.explanation: IAS (Indicated Airspeed): what the ASI reads. CAS (Calibrated Airspeed): IAS corrected for instrument and position errors (from POH calibration tables). TAS (True Airspeed): CAS corrected for non-standard density (altitude and temperature) — the actual speed through the air mass. TAS is approximately 2% greater than CAS per 1000 ft of altitude.

questions.q: What is compass rose on a sectional chart and what is its purpose?
choices: A decorative element with no navigational purpose
Magnetic reference roses printed around VOR stations showing magnetic degrees to assist in interpreting VOR radials relative to surrounding terrain and airspace features
A rose-shaped symbol indicating a helicopter landing pad
A symbol for a rotating beacon at an airport
questions.explanation: Compass roses printed around VOR stations on sectional charts show magnetic degrees clockwise from magnetic north. They help pilots visualize VOR radials and assist in interpreting CDI indications relative to landmarks and airspace on the chart.

questions.q: What is a course deviation indicator (CDI) and how does it display VOR guidance?
choices: CDI is the Course Direction Indicator — only used on GPS

The CDI displays how far left or right the aircraft is from the selected course (OBS setting). Each dot of deflection = 2 degrees off course. The course line is the OBS setting; the needle deflects toward the course to re-intercept.
CDI always displays bearing to the station
CDI only works within 5 nm of the VOR station
questions.explanation: The CDI displays the aircraft's lateral displacement from the selected VOR course (OBS setting). When the needle deflects right, the course is to the right — fly right to center it. Each dot = approximately 2 degrees off course. Full-scale deflection = 10 degrees off course. The OBS sets which radial or course is selected.

questions.q: What is a Class B VFR transition route?
choices: A high-altitude route above Class B for jet traffic

Specific published routes through Class B airspace on VFR Terminal Area Charts that VFR aircraft may use at specified altitudes — NOT a clearance; pilots must receive ATC authorization to use these routes
A VFR route around Class B airspace — same as a flyway
A VFR route only for turboprops and jets
questions.explanation: VFR transition routes are published on TAC charts and provide specific routes through Class B airspace at designated altitudes. However, VFR pilots must STILL receive ATC clearance to use these routes — they are not clearance-free corridors. The term 'transition route' on a TAC indicates a commonly used path that ATC is familiar with.

questions.q: How does the E6B flight computer assist with fuel planning and why is it important to use actual data?
choices: The E6B cannot assist with fuel planning — it is only for wind calculations

The E6B (or electronic equivalent) can calculate fuel burn based on fuel flow rate and time enroute — but accurate results require using actual POH fuel flow figures for the specific power setting and altitude planned, not standard estimates
The E6B automatically downloads fuel prices from FBOs
E6B fuel calculations are not accepted by the FAA
questions.explanation: The E6B flight computer can calculate fuel requirements (fuel flow x time = fuel burn). The critical factor is using accurate POH fuel consumption data for the specific power setting and altitude planned. Using manufacturer estimates without accounting for actual power settings or a fuel-enriched mixture leads to dangerous fuel planning errors.

questions.q: What is a compass locator and how is it used for navigation?
choices: A handheld compass used to find the airport
A low or medium frequency (LF/MF) NDB (Non-Directional Beacon) co-located with an ILS outer or middle marker — used with an ADF receiver to home to the beacon
A magnetic compass installed in the control tower
A GPS waypoint near an airport
questions.explanation: A compass locator is a low-power NDB (Non-Directional Beacon) co-located with ILS outer markers (LOM) or middle markers (LMM). It operates on 200-415 kHz and can be received with an ADF (Automatic Direction Finder). Compass locators are used for instrument approaches and as a backup navaid.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a course line on a sectional chart and how is it used for flight planning?
choices: It is drawn by ATC to show your cleared route

A straight line drawn from departure to destination on the sectional; the pilot measures the true course with a plotter, applies TVMDC to get compass heading, and identifies checkpoints, terrain, and airspace along the route
It shows restricted areas only
Course lines are not used in VFR flight planning
questions.explanation: For VFR flight planning, pilots draw a course line on the sectional chart from departure to destination. Using a plotter, they measure the true course angle. Applying TVMDC gives the compass heading. The line also identifies terrain, airspace, checkpoints, and emergency landing areas along the route.

questions.q: How do you identify a VOR station before using it for navigation?
choices: Visual inspection of the VOR antenna from the aircraft
Tune the VOR frequency and listen for the three-letter Morse code identifier or voice identification; no identifier (or TST signal) means the VOR is unreliable and should not be used for navigation
Use GPS to confirm the VOR location first
Call ATC to confirm the VOR is active
questions.explanation: Before using a VOR, identify it by listening to the Morse code three-letter identifier on the VOR frequency. A test (TST) signal means the VOR is under maintenance and unreliable. No identifier may mean the station is out of service. Always identify before navigating.

questions.q: What is the approximate rule of thumb for the effect of a 10-knot headwind on takeoff distance?
choices: No meaningful effect
Reduces takeoff distance by approximately 10%
Reduces takeoff distance by approximately 20%
Increases takeoff distance by 10%
questions.explanation: A 10-knot headwind reduces takeoff distance by approximately 10%. Conversely, a 2-knot tailwind can increase takeoff distance by approximately 10%. Always apply performance chart corrections for actual wind conditions.

questions.q: What is service ceiling?
choices: The maximum altitude the aircraft can ever reach with any engine power
The altitude at which the maximum rate of climb equals 100 fpm
The maximum altitude for VFR cruise flight
The altitude above which pressurization is required
questions.explanation: Service ceiling is the density altitude at which the aircraft's maximum rate of climb equals 100 fpm. Absolute ceiling is where the rate of climb reaches 0 fpm. Both decrease with excess weight and high DA.

questions.q: POH takeoff performance charts assume which runway conditions?
choices: Grass runway at sea level on a standard day
Paved, level, dry runway at the charted conditions with maximum braking
Any runway type with no special corrections needed
Uphill slope with a headwind component
questions.explanation: POH performance charts assume: paved, level, dry runway; correct technique; maximum certificated gross weight; and standard atmospheric corrections as charted. Real-world conditions such as grass, slope, or wet runway require additional safety margins.

questions.q: A crosswind of 90 degrees to the runway at 20 knots — what is the crosswind component?
choices: 10 knots
15 knots
20 knots
Zero — pure crosswind has no runway component
questions.explanation: When the wind is exactly 90 degrees to the runway, the full wind speed is a crosswind component. Crosswind component = wind speed x sin(angle off runway). Sin(90) = 1.0, so 20 kts x 1.0 = 20 kts crosswind.

questions.q: The density altitude at an airport is 7,000 ft but the field elevation is 4,000 ft MSL. What does this mean for takeoff performance?
choices: The aircraft will perform as if it were at 4,000 ft — DA has no practical effect
The aircraft will perform as if taking off from a 7,000-ft elevation airport — significantly degraded performance
DA only affects climb performance, not ground roll
DA only matters above 5,000 ft MSL
questions.explanation: Density altitude represents the effective altitude from a performance standpoint. An aircraft at 7,000 ft DA will have the engine power, propeller efficiency, and aerodynamic performance of an aircraft at 7,000 ft MSL regardless of the actual field elevation.

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of filing a VFR flight plan?
choices: It is required by law for all cross-country flights over 50 nm
To provide search and rescue information if the aircraft is overdue — it is NOT legally required but strongly recommended
To get an ATC transponder code for radar identification
To reserve airspace for the flight route
questions.explanation: VFR flight plans are NOT required by 14 CFR but are strongly recommended as a search-and-rescue tool. If not closed within 30 minutes of the ETA, SAR procedures may begin. Always close the flight plan after landing by contacting FSS.

questions.q: What does RAIM stand for and why does it matter for IFR GPS use?
choices: Radar Altitude Instrument Monitor — used to verify altimeter accuracy

Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring — the GPS self-checks signal integrity using redundant satellites. If RAIM is unavailable, GPS cannot be used as a primary navigation source for IFR approaches without WAAS.
Route Approved Instrument Minimums — an FAA designation for approach procedures
Real-time Airspace Information Monitoring — a system for TFR alerts
questions.explanation: RAIM allows a GPS receiver to check its own integrity by comparing signals from multiple satellites. For IFR non-WAAS GPS approaches, a RAIM check must be done before departure. If RAIM is unavailable during the approach, the GPS cannot be used as the primary instrument. WAAS-equipped GPS does not require a RAIM check.

questions.q: How do you request VFR flight following from ATC?
choices: File a VFR flight plan and ATC automatically provides flight following
Contact the appropriate ARTCC or approach control on the appropriate frequency: identify yourself, location, altitude, destination, and request radar traffic advisories
Squawk 1200 and ATC automatically provides radar service
Flight following is only available for IFR aircraft
questions.explanation: To request VFR flight following: contact the appropriate approach control or Center on the correct frequency. State: aircraft identification, aircraft type, position, altitude, destination, and request. ATC will assign a discrete transponder code. Service is workload-permitting and can be terminated by ATC.

questions.q: What transponder codes indicate specific situations?
choices: 7500 = lost communications; 7600 = emergency; 7700 = hijacking
7500 = hijacking; 7600 = lost communications; 7700 = general emergency
7500 = general emergency; 7600 = hijacking; 7700 = radio failure
All emergency codes are the same — 7700 covers all situations
questions.explanation: 7500 = hijacking/unlawful interference. 7600 = radio/communications failure. 7700 = general emergency. These codes are internationally recognized and immediately alert ATC to your situation. Squawk 1200 = VFR flight without flight following.

questions.q: When lost, what is the recommended first action — and what do the '5 C's' stand for?
choices: Immediately declare an emergency on 121.5 MHz

Climb, Conserve, Communicate, Consult, Circle. First action is to climb for better radio/radar reception and visual range, then conserve fuel, communicate with ATC, consult all available navigation, and circle while resolving position.
Land immediately at the nearest airport regardless of conditions
Continue on course and hope to identify a landmark
questions.explanation: The 5 C's for lost procedure: Climb (altitude improves radio contact and visual range), Conserve fuel, Communicate (121.5 or ATC), Confess (tell ATC you are lost), Comply (with ATC instructions). Climbing is the first priority as it enables all other actions.

questions.q: When diverting to an alternate airport in flight, what information do you need to compute?
choices: Only the new heading — everything else can wait until landing

New magnetic course, estimated time enroute (ETE), fuel required for the diversion plus reserve, and whether the alternate has appropriate services for your needs. Communicate intentions to ATC promptly.
Only the fuel remaining — heading can be determined by GPS
The diversion procedure only applies to IFR flights
questions.explanation: When diverting: (1) identify a suitable alternate on the chart, (2) estimate the new magnetic course using the TVMDC formula, (3) estimate distance, (4) calculate ETE = distance / groundspeed, (5) calculate fuel required + reserve, (6) communicate with ATC. GPS simplifies step 2-4 greatly but understand the manual method.

questions.q: What is WAAS and how does it improve GPS accuracy?
choices: Wind Adjusted Approach System — adjusts approaches for wind conditions

Wide Area Augmentation System — a network of ground reference stations that transmit corrections to GPS satellites, improving accuracy to approximately 3 meters or better. Enables GPS LPV approaches comparable to ILS.
Weather Avoidance Advisory System — weather overlay for EFBs
Waypoint Automated Alerting System — warns of nearby waypoints
questions.explanation: WAAS (Wide Area Augmentation System) uses a network of ground reference stations throughout the US to compute GPS signal corrections, which are uplinked to geostationary satellites and broadcast to WAAS-capable receivers. WAAS improves GPS accuracy to approximately 1-3 meters and enables LPV approaches with decision heights as low as 200 ft HAT.
